compiled_state.author: RebarBotNava
compiled_state.description:
## Context
Add an `rebar.authorship.v1` attestation KIND on the foundation asymmetric
attest substrate (`src/rebar/attest/`), so an event can be cryptographically
signed by an author identity and verified against that identity's in-band public
keys. This is the signing/verifying MACHINERY (registration + sign/verify + trust
root); WIRING it into the write path and enforcement is a later leaf (3183). Builds
on ddbe (identity entity holds `keys`) and the foundation SSHSIG scheme.

## Why
Authorship certification needs a tamper-evident proof that a specific identity
authored an event, verifiable offline against public keys carried in-band on the
identity entity (no external PKI). Reusing the foundation `attest` substrate gives
policy-pinned scheme selection (closes the alg-confusion class) for free.

## What
### 1. New module `src/rebar/attest/authorship.py`
Constants: `AUTHORSHIP_KIND = "rebar.authorship.v1"`,
`AUTHORSHIP_NAMESPACE = "rebar.authorship.v1"`,
`PAYLOAD_TYPE = "application/vnd.rebar.authorship.v1+json"`.

- `register_authorship_policy() -> None` (idempotent): pins
  `registry.POLICY[AUTHORSHIP_KIND] = registry.Policy(scheme="sshsig",
  namespace=AUTHORSHIP_NAMESPACE)`. (The `sshsig` SCHEME is already registered by
  `sshsig.register_sshsig_scheme()`; this pins the KIND→scheme policy so
  `registry.verify` selects the scheme from source-pinned policy, never from the
  envelope.) Wire the call into `src/rebar/attest/__init__.py` right after the
  existing `sshsig.register_sshsig_scheme()` line, so it is live at import.
- `sign_authorship(payload: bytes, key_path: str, principal: str) -> dsse.Envelope`:
  FIRST call `sshsig.ensure_available()` (raises `sshsig.SshKeygenUnavailable`
  when ssh-keygen is missing/too old — the honest precondition; without this
  `sshsig.sign`'s `subprocess.run(check=True)` would instead raise
  `FileNotFoundError`/`CalledProcessError`). Then `pae = dsse.pae(PAYLOAD_TYPE, payload)`; `sig = sshsig.sign(pae, key_path,
  AUTHORSHIP_NAMESPACE)`; return `dsse.Envelope(PAYLOAD_TYPE, payload,
  [dsse.Signature(keyid=principal, sig=sig)])`. `principal` is the author identity's
  id (it becomes the SSHSIG `-I` principal — `SshsigScheme.verify` reads
  `signatures[0].keyid` as the principal). Raises `sshsig.SshKeygenUnavailable` if
  ssh-keygen is missing/too old (do not swallow — signing must be honest).
- `allowed_signers_from_keys(keys: list[str], principal: str) -> str`: build SSHSIG
  allowed_signers content — one line per key, `"<principal> <authorized-keys-line>"`
  (the identity's `keys` are OpenSSH authorized-keys lines like
  `"ssh-ed25519 AAAA..."`; prefixing the principal yields a valid allowed_signers
  entry). Join with `"\n"`. Skip blank/malformed key entries.
- `resolve_trust_root(identity_id: str, *, repo_root=None) -> str | None`: load the
  identity via `rebar.show_ticket(identity_id, repo_root=...)`; if it is an
  `identity` ticket with a non-empty `keys` list, return
  `allowed_signers_from_keys(keys, principal=identity_id)`; else `None` (unknown
  identity or no keys → nothing to trust).
- `verify_authorship(envelope: dsse.Envelope, identity_id: str, *, repo_root=None)
  -> registry.Verdict`: `trust_root = resolve_trust_root(identity_id, ...)`; if
  `None`, return a non-verified `registry.Verdict(False, "unknown_principal",
  reason=...)`; else return `registry.verify(AUTHORSHIP_KIND, envelope,
  trust_root)`. The envelope's signature `keyid` MUST equal `identity_id` (the
  principal) or SSHSIG verification fails — this is what makes "signed by A does not
  verify against B" hold: verifying A's envelope against B builds B's allowed_signers
  (B's keys, principal=B_id), and neither the key nor the principal matches.

### 2. Determinism / trust-root selection
Trust root is ALWAYS resolved from the identity entity's in-band `keys` (never from
the envelope), and the scheme is ALWAYS selected from source-pinned
`POLICY[AUTHORSHIP_KIND]`. A caller cannot smuggle a scheme or a trust anchor
through the envelope.

## Scope
In: the authorship module (constants, policy registration, sign, verify, trust-root
resolution, allowed_signers builder) and its registration in `attest/__init__.py`.
Out: signing real events at the write seam / storing the envelope on events
(3183 enforcement), key lifecycle & rotation (e165). `payload` here is arbitrary
bytes supplied by the caller (later the event's canonical bytes); bde5 does not
decide what bytes an event contributes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `register_authorship_policy()` pins `POLICY["rebar.authorship.v1"]` to
  `Policy(scheme="sshsig", namespace="rebar.authorship.v1")` and is invoked at
  `attest` package import; a test asserts `registry.resolve("rebar.authorship.v1")`
  returns that policy.
- [ ] Round-trip: given an ssh keypair whose PUBLIC key is stored on identity A's
  `keys`, `sign_authorship(payload, privkey, principal=A_id)` produces an envelope
  that `verify_authorship(envelope, A_id)` reports `verified=True`
  (test skips if `ssh-keygen` unavailable via `sshsig.ensure_available`).
- [ ] Cross-identity rejection: the SAME envelope verified against a DIFFERENT
  identity B (different keypair) reports `verified=False` (B's in-band keys are the
  trust root, and neither key nor principal matches).
- [ ] Tamper rejection: mutating the envelope `payload` (or a signature byte) makes
  `verify_authorship(..., A_id)` report `verified=False`.
- [ ] Unknown/keyless identity: `verify_authorship` against an id that is not an
  `identity` ticket, or one with empty `keys`, returns a non-verified Verdict
  (never raises).

## file_impact
- `src/rebar/attest/authorship.py` (new: constants, register_authorship_policy, sign_authorship, allowed_signers_from_keys, resolve_trust_root, verify_authorship)
- `src/rebar/attest/__init__.py` (call register_authorship_policy() at import, after sshsig registration)

file_impact.reason: new authorship kind: policy registration + sign/verify + trust-root from identity keys

compiled_state.title: identity: authorship attestation kind on the foundation substrate
